items.name: awesome-openclaw-skills
items.summary: Large OpenClaw skill collection sourced from registry
items.install_cmd: OpenClaw is a locally-running AI assistant that operates directly on your machine. Skills extend its capabilities, allowing it to interact with external services, automate workflows, and perform specialized tasks. This collection helps you discover and install the right skills for your needs. It can also serve as a source of inspiration for OpenClaw use cases.
items.use_case: Find and compare skills quickly before deciding what to install or audit.
items.risk_explanation: Medium risk: likely includes executable workflow logic, automation, or external tool interaction; review before broad promotion.
items.why_include_now: Strong ecosystem signal, clear skill identity, and good value as an index anchor for the first Skill Tree library.
items.direct_value_to_skill_tree: Improves trust and coverage of a major category users will search for on day one.

safety_precheck.notes: Contains executable skill logic or automation; needs repo-level audit before inclusion.

workflow_risk_signals: governance depth is unclear, so runtime controls may need manual review

items.name: awesome-claude-code
items.summary: A curated list of awesome skills, hooks, slash-commands, agent orchestrators, applications, and plugins for Claude Code by Anthropic
items.use_case: Use when one agent is not enough and you need orchestrated, multi-step, or delegated execution.
items.risk_explanation: Medium risk: likely involves executable automation, external tools, or broader workflow authority; review setup and permissions first.
items.install_friction: needs credentials/auth setup; requires Node/npm tooling; may require Python environment; may require MCP/server config; may require Docker or local services; review permissions before broad use
items.governance_rationale: repository signals support for scheduled/background or long-running workflows; repository mentions multi-agent, delegation, or orchestration patterns; repository references governance, security, permissions, or audit controls; protocol/integration hints found: MCP, CLI
items.runtime_control_rationale: distribution layer looks like discovery / registry; background execution appears supported; local-first posture appears strong; workflow lock-in assessed as medium; permission visibility assessed as high
items.execution_boundary: Primarily discovery/index layer; execution usually happens only after the user enters downstream skill repos or tools. Includes delegated/multi-agent coordination, so effective execution may span more than one agent layer.
items.where_code_runs: Mostly local reading/browsing until user installs a downstream skill; actual code execution is deferred to linked tools. MCP support suggests standardized external tool/server handoff is possible.
items.secret_holder: Usually user-held in downstream tools, not in the registry layer itself.
items.audit_evidence: README/docs, repo structure, and linked downstream installation paths provide the main audit evidence. Permission/governance visibility appears relatively strong from available metadata.
items.background_duration_fit: long; repository signals support for unattended or background-style execution.
items.execution_evidence: Registry/readme metadata, linked repos, and installation/docs paths are the main evidence surfaces. Multi-agent/delegation signals suggest checking handoff logs and execution trace visibility.
items.takeover_path: User can usually stop at selection time by not installing or invoking downstream skills; takeover is strongest before execution starts.
items.scheduled_fit_explanation: Suitable for Scheduled page because it signals repeatable, automatable, or long-running workflow patterns rather than one-off prompt usage.
items.why_include_now: Strong ecosystem signal, clear skill identity, and good value as an index anchor for the first Skill Tree library.
items.direct_value_to_skill_tree: Improves trust and coverage of a major category users will search for on day one.

safety_precheck.notes: Mostly registry/spec/content or local workflow scope; low obvious abuse surface from description.

workflow_entry_signals: choose when work must run on a schedule or without a person watching every step
choose when the job spans multiple checkpoints, retries, or context handoffs
choose when one agent is not enough and you need delegation or orchestration
choose when the agent must load external context or connect through MCP/API/CLI bridges
workflow_risk_signals: unattended execution can amplify mistakes before a human notices
delegation broadens blast radius and makes failures harder to trace
protocol bridges widen the integration and credential surface area
